Aiming to generate more money annually while also maintaining an ample stock of residential and downtown parking spaces, SFMTA recently became more strategic, using data to decide where to deploy officers, empowering them with license plate-reader technology and increasing the number who work on weekends. While some motorists have accused the city of a “war on cars,” SFMTA officials have expressed pride in the results. The agency is trying to patch a $322 million deficit, and any revenue stream helps.
When he posted [the link on social media](https://x.com/rtwlz/status/1970536901733130741) Tuesday morning, it swiftly went viral with more than 1 million views.

excerpts:
How it works
Walz said he spent about $100 and a couple of weekends working on Bop Spotter, which he installed using a friend's ladder in the middle of the day on Sept. 28.
"Bop Spotter's like a living time capsule in real time of what songs are playing," said Bop Spotter's creator, Riley Walz.
San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.
The project is resonating. People in places like New York, Boston and Berlin have gotten in touch about creating their own Bop Spotters.

It's like the fun part of surveillance capitalism. We're all being monitored all the time — by our phones, by cameras. But this is a way to take that, flip it, and pull something that's fun out of it.

**Bop Spotter is capturing the vibe of a single street corner** **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

A solar panel on top of the box keeps the phone powered up. A microphone on the bottom captures songs it hears nearby and logs them on the Bop Spotter [website](https://walzr.com/bop-spotter) . The system runs on the neighborhood's free public Wi-Fi network.
The system logs about 150 tracks a day by an array of artists including Nipsey Hussle, Celine Dion, The Temptations and Peso Pluma.
citations.title: An old phone in a plastic box captures the cultural vibes of a San Francisco neighborhood

excerpts: z installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it "Bop Spotter," after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots.

excerpts: AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention.

A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ("You Only Look Once") watches the video feed continously, and draws a box around every pedestrian it sees. excerpts: The serial website builder Riley Walz launched a project that tracked San Francisco parking enforcement in real time—until the public data feed was cut off.

“In rare lightning speed, the SF government changed their site within hours of this site going live,” reads an announcement Walz added to Find My Parking Cops on Tuesday afternoon. “I can’t get data from it anymore.”

/status/1970536901733130741) on social media Tuesday, the same day as its initial release. It enjoyed a few good hours, before the site’s functionality vanished: City officials appear to have cut off the sources of data that Walz was using to track parking enforcement.
“

The idea for Find My Parking Cops was first sparked when Walz’s roommate got a parking fine. Walz noticed the nine-digit identification number on the ticket and started looking for patterns. By using publicly available information, he believed he cracked the code.
The site kept tabs on where city workers were issuing tickets, showing the locations of parking control officers on a city map in real time.
“I worked in my free time on the weekends the last few weeks to make it happen,” he says.
“I'm not ‘pro’ parking cop. I'm not ‘anti’ parking cop,” says Walz. “It's just data I was able to unearth, and I thought it would be cool to visualize it.”

excerpts: So no ticket can end in 7, 8, or 9.
So the ticket after 984,946,606 is actually 984,946,610, and after that is 984,946,621.
Only God knows why, but I assume this is a remnant of an old system.

I also discovered the devices that parking cops use probably claim IDs in batches of 100. So if an officer just wrote a ticket, I know with certainty the ID after that one will be the next ticket the same officer writes.
AND, immediately after a ticket is written, it becomes available to be viewed on the city's website.
With all of this knowledge, I wrote a scraper that works extremely efficiently and stores parking tickets almost immediately after they are written.
Because there are about 300 parking cops, there are about 300 incomplete batches of 100 tickets.
All I have to do is check the first ticket in each batch I don't have in my database. This approach means I only have to make a request to the city's website every few seconds. Great!

How it works
Walz said he spent about $100 and a couple of weekends working on Bop Spotter, which he installed using a friend's ladder in the middle of the day on Sept. 28.
"Bop Spotter's like a living time capsule in real time of what songs are playing," said Bop Spotter's creator, Riley Walz.
San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.
The project is resonating. People in places like New York, Boston and Berlin have gotten in touch about creating their own Bop Spotters.

It's like the fun part of surveillance capitalism. We're all being monitored all the time — by our phones, by cameras. But this is a way to take that, flip it, and pull something that's fun out of it.

excerpts: Walz had launched his project out of curiosity, at a moment when the SFMTA is [ramping up citations](https://www.sfchronicle.com/opinion/emilyhoeven/article/parking-excelsior-san-francisco-20791982.php) for everything from [blocking driveways](https://www.sfchronicle.com/sf/article/car-driveway-san-francisco-20763724.php) to overstaying at meters, to commandeering bus stops. The agency is trying to patch a $322 million deficit, and any revenue stream helps.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

excerpts: San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission
"This is culture surveillance, where I'm picking up on people's activities down here," Walz said. "But it's just music."

A solar panel on top of the box keeps the phone powered up. A microphone on the bottom captures songs it hears nearby and logs them on the Bop Spotter [website](https://walzr.com/bop-spotter) . The system runs on the neighborhood's free public Wi-Fi network.
The system logs about 150 tracks a day by an array of artists including Nipsey Hussle, Celine Dion, The Temptations and Peso Pluma.

**Bop Spotter is capturing the vibe of a single street corner** **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.
Walz said he named Bop Spotter after ShotSpotter, a surveillance technology police departments use to track the location of gunshots. But he's not trying to make a political point.

excerpts: **Bop Spotter is capturing the vibe of a single street corner** **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.
"This is culture surveillance, where I'm picking up on people's activities down here," Walz said. "But it's just music."
Walz said he named Bop Spotter after ShotSpotter, a surveillance technology police departments use to track the location of gunshots. But he's not trying to make a political point.

A solar panel on top of the box keeps the phone powered up. A microphone on the bottom captures songs it hears nearby and logs them on the Bop Spotter [website](https://walzr.com/bop-spotter) . The system runs on the neighborhood's free public Wi-Fi network.
The system logs about 150 tracks a day by an array of artists including Nipsey Hussle, Celine Dion, The Temptations and Peso Pluma.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

A solar panel on top of the box keeps the phone powered up. A microphone on the bottom captures songs it hears nearby and logs them on the Bop Spotter [website](https://walzr.com/bop-spotter) . The system runs on the neighborhood's free public Wi-Fi network.

excerpts: AI is watching this Manhattan doorbell camera, counting the people walking by. It counts what they're wearing and if they have an umbrella. Sometimes the best forecast is just paying attention.

As people walk through the frame, it assigns each one a unique ID. It can follow people across multiple video frames, even if they briefly disappear behind someone else.

excerpts: The serial website builder Riley Walz launched a project that tracked San Francisco parking enforcement in real time—until the public data feed was cut off.

The idea for Find My Parking Cops was first sparked when Walz’s roommate got a parking fine. Walz noticed the nine-digit identification number on the ticket and started looking for patterns. By using publicly available information, he believed he cracked the code.
The site kept tabs on where city workers were issuing tickets, showing the locations of parking control officers on a city map in real time.

“In rare lightning speed, the SF government changed their site within hours of this site going live,” reads an announcement Walz added to Find My Parking Cops on Tuesday afternoon. “I can’t get data from it anymore.”

/status/1970536901733130741) on social media Tuesday, the same day as its initial release. It enjoyed a few good hours, before the site’s functionality vanished: City officials appear to have cut off the sources of data that Walz was using to track parking enforcement.

“I worked in my free time on the weekends the last few weeks to make it happen,” he says.

excerpts: In 2023, I ordered $1 cans of beans to a bunch of extended family members. It ignited our family group chat for a few weeks and everyone was super into it. Pictures of beans started rolling in. Then _they_ started sending random stuff to each other. An asbestos warning label, cookies, a tin of sardines. Someone even sent a pregnancy test to my grandmother.

# Postal
Arbitrage
As of 2025, a stamp for a letter costs $0.78 in the United States. Amazon Prime sells items for less than that... **with free shipping!** Why send a postcard when you can send actual stuff?
This site is not affiliated with or endorsed by Amazon, obviously!
I found all items under $0.78 with free Prime shipping — screws, cans, pasta, whatever. Add a free gift note. It arrives in 1 or 2 days. Done.

excerpts: “I worked in my free time on the weekends the last few weeks to make it happen,” he says.
The serial website builder Riley Walz launched a project that tracked San Francisco parking enforcement in real time—until the public data feed was cut off.

The idea for Find My Parking Cops was first sparked when Walz’s roommate got a parking fine. Walz noticed the nine-digit identification number on the ticket and started looking for patterns. By using publicly available information, he believed he cracked the code.
The site kept tabs on where city workers were issuing tickets, showing the locations of parking control officers on a city map in real time.
“I'm not ‘pro’ parking cop. I'm not ‘anti’ parking cop,” says Walz. “It's just data I was able to unearth, and I thought it would be cool to visualize it.”

excerpts: San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.
"Bop Spotter's like a living time capsule in real time of what songs are playing," said Bop Spotter's creator, Riley Walz.

How it works
Walz said he spent about $100 and a couple of weekends working on Bop Spotter, which he installed using a friend's ladder in the middle of the day on Sept. 28.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

It's like the fun part of surveillance capitalism. We're all being monitored all the time — by our phones, by cameras. But this is a way to take that, flip it, and pull something that's fun out of it.

Walz had launched his project out of curiosity, at a moment when the SFMTA is [ramping up citations](https://www.sfchronicle.com/opinion/emilyhoeven/article/parking-excelsior-san-francisco-20791982.php) for everything from [blocking driveways](https://www.sfchronicle.com/sf/article/car-driveway-san-francisco-20763724.php) to overstaying at meters, to commandeering bus stops. Aiming to generate more money annually while also maintaining an ample stock of residential and downtown parking spaces, SFMTA recently became more strategic, using data to decide where to deploy officers, empowering them with license plate-reader technology and increasing the number who work on weekends. While some motorists have accused the city of a “war on cars,” SFMTA officials have expressed pride in the results. The agency is trying to patch a $322 million deficit, and any revenue stream helps.

excerpts: The idea for Find My Parking Cops was first sparked when Walz’s roommate got a parking fine. Walz noticed the nine-digit identification number on the ticket and started looking for patterns. By using publicly available information, he believed he cracked the code.
The site kept tabs on where city workers were issuing tickets, showing the locations of parking control officers on a city map in real time.

“In rare lightning speed, the SF government changed their site within hours of this site going live,” reads an announcement Walz added to Find My Parking Cops on Tuesday afternoon. “I can’t get data from it anymore.”
The serial website builder Riley Walz launched a project that tracked San Francisco parking enforcement in real time—until the public data feed was cut off.
“I worked in my free time on the weekends the last few weeks to make it happen,” he says.

excerpts: A simple person-detection AI model called YOLO ("You Only Look Once") watches the video feed continously, and draws a box around every pedestrian it sees. excerpts: **Bop Spotter is capturing the vibe of a single street corner** **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

A solar panel on top of the box keeps the phone powered up. A microphone on the bottom captures songs it hears nearby and logs them on the Bop Spotter [website](https://walzr.com/bop-spotter) . The system runs on the neighborhood's free public Wi-Fi network.

excerpts:
How it works
Walz said he spent about $100 and a couple of weekends working on Bop Spotter, which he installed using a friend's ladder in the middle of the day on Sept. 28.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

**Bop Spotter is capturing the vibe of a single street corner** **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

It's like the fun part of surveillance capitalism. We're all being monitored all the time — by our phones, by cameras. But this is a way to take that, flip it, and pull something that's fun out of it.
San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.
"Bop Spotter's like a living time capsule in real time of what songs are playing," said Bop Spotter's creator, Riley Walz.

excerpts: The idea for Find My Parking Cops was first sparked when Walz’s roommate got a parking fine. Walz noticed the nine-digit identification number on the ticket and started looking for patterns. By using publicly available information, he believed he cracked the code.
The serial website builder Riley Walz launched a project that tracked San Francisco parking enforcement in real time—until the public data feed was cut off.
The site kept tabs on where city workers were issuing tickets, showing the locations of parking control officers on a city map in real time.
“I worked in my free time on the weekends the last few weeks to make it happen,” he says.
“I'm not ‘pro’ parking cop. I'm not ‘anti’ parking cop,” says Walz. “It's just data I was able to unearth, and I thought it would be cool to visualize it.”

LooksMapping was created by Riley Walz, a 22-year-old programmer in San Francisco with a penchant for using Google review data to make sardonic observations about the restaurant industry.

[Riley Walz](https://walzr.com/) , the same app-maker behind [Bop Spotter](https://walzr.com/bop-spotter) — which uses Shazam to pick up passing tunes from a mystery location in the Mission District — and [LooksMapping](https://sfist.com/2025/09/17/asinine-website-ranks-sf-restaurant-patrons-by-hotness-using-ai/) , the restaurant "hotness" meter, has a pretty useful new app called [Find My Parking Cops](https://walzr.com/sf-parking/) , which maps out the shift routes of every SF parking enforcement officer by the tickets they write, every day.
citations.title: Local App-Maker Makes Viral App to Track SF Parking Cops - SFist

excerpts: San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.
"Bop Spotter's like a living time capsule in real time of what songs are playing," said Bop Spotter's creator, Riley Walz.

**Bop Spotter is capturing the vibe of a single street corner** **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

How it works
Walz said he spent about $100 and a couple of weekends working on Bop Spotter, which he installed using a friend's ladder in the middle of the day on Sept. 28.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

It's like the fun part of surveillance capitalism. We're all being monitored all the time — by our phones, by cameras. But this is a way to take that, flip it, and pull something that's fun out of it.
citations.title: An old phone in a plastic box captures the cultural vibes of a San Francisco neighborhood

content.executive_summary: Riley Walz's model for idea generation, project selection, and execution is a dynamic and opportunistic process rooted in curiosity, rapid development, and public performance. His ideas often originate from observing peculiar data sources, systemic inefficiencies, or cultural quirks in the digital and physical world, rather than from formal problem-solving. He maintains an idea backlog, evidenced by a physical wall of Post-it notes and the sheer volume of his output, from which he selects projects based on a mix of personal interest, technical feasibility, low cost, and high potential for social circulation and media attention. Walz's execution philosophy is defined by the 'Minimum Viable Stunt': he scopes projects to be built quickly, often over a weekend, and ships a minimal but functional version to gauge public reaction. Data is acquired through a variety of ad-hoc methods, including scraping public websites, reverse-engineering unofficial APIs, and deploying simple physical sensors. His projects are launched with clear, descriptive names and transparent 'Methodology' pages, a practice that builds trust and fuels media narratives. This entire cycle is geared towards creating novel, often provocative, and shareable experiences that function as both technical experiments and cultural commentary, ultimately leveraging public attention as a form of validation and a potential catalyst for career opportunities, such as his hiring by OpenAI.
key_heuristics_summary: Idea Sourcing - 'Data First' Approach: Generate ideas by discovering interesting, overlooked, or exploitable data sources, such as public government systems, large web platforms (Google Maps), pricing anomalies (Amazon), or physical-world signals (street audio).

Idea Generation - Reverse-Engineering and Exploitation: Actively probe and reverse-engineer public systems (like SFMTA's ticket numbering) to extract data not available through official APIs. Exploit systemic inefficiencies or logistical quirks (like postal arbitrage).

Idea Generation - Playful Sensing and Observation: Deploy low-cost physical sensors (like a phone with Shazam for 'Bop Spotter') or use AI on public video feeds ('Weather Watching') to capture and publish real-time data about the physical world, driven by curiosity.

Project Selection - Prioritize Curiosity and Novelty: Choose projects that are personally interesting, fun, or answer a curious question ('I just wanted to see what would happen'). The potential for a project to be novel, provocative, or absurd is a key selection criterion.

Project Selection - Assess for Virality and Press Potential: Favor ideas with a high likelihood of generating social media buzz and press coverage due to their cleverness, controversy, or clear utility.

Project Scoping - The 'Minimum Viable Stunt': Scope projects to a minimal set of features that demonstrate the core concept effectively. The goal is to ship a functional proof-of-concept quickly, not a polished, long-term product.

Execution - 'Ship Fast' and Iterate Based on Reaction: Build and launch projects rapidly, often over a weekend. Use public reaction and feedback as the primary signal for whether to iterate, expand, or abandon a project.

Data Acquisition - Pragmatic and Unconventional: Use whatever technical means necessary to acquire data, from simple scraping and official APIs to reverse-engineering endpoints and deploying physical sensors. Acknowledge but operate within the gray areas of Terms of Service.

Communication - Descriptive Naming and Framing: Use clear, direct, and often punny or referential names (e.g., 'Bop Spotter', 'Find My Parking Cops') that immediately convey the project's function and tone.

Communication - 'Show Your Work' Transparency: Consistently publish 'Methodology' or 'How it works' pages that detail the technical implementation, data sources, and even potential biases or flaws. This builds trust, satisfies curiosity, and provides a narrative for media.

Risk Mitigation - Strategic Opacity: While transparent about methods, strategically withhold specific operational details (like the exact location of a sensor) to prevent projects from being easily shut down by authorities or platforms.

idea_generation_heuristics.description: This heuristic involves identifying and exploiting technical patterns, predictable numbering schemes, or vulnerabilities in publicly accessible data systems (often governmental) to extract and re-present information in a novel, real-time, or more accessible format. It's characterized by a willingness to explore undocumented or non-official data streams.

This method involves deploying simple, low-cost physical sensors or consumer devices (like a phone with a microphone) in the physical world to continuously capture and publish real-time environmental or social data. The motivation is often described as playful curiosity or creating a 'funny little joke' to see what patterns emerge from public life.

This approach consists of scraping massive public datasets, such as user-generated content from platforms like Google Maps, and then applying artificial intelligence or machine learning models to analyze the data and derive unexpected, novel, or often provocative insights and classifications.

This heuristic focuses on discovering and leveraging pricing discrepancies, logistical quirks, or other inefficiencies within established commercial or public services. The goal is to create creative 'arbitrage' opportunities or social experiments, often with a humorous or satirical angle.

This involves taking institutional data that is technically public but practically obscure (like library checkout logs) and making it easily accessible and observable. The aim is to reveal community patterns, provide transparency, or simply satisfy curiosity about public activities.

A specific application of physical-world sensing, this method uses AI-powered computer vision models (like YOLO) on publicly available video feeds (such as doorbell cameras or webcams) to automatically collect and analyze real-world behavioral or environmental data.

This involves sourcing and presenting overlooked or unintentionally public digital content, such as YouTube videos with default filenames. The goal is to create a platform that offers raw, unedited, and authentic glimpses into everyday life and digital history.

A foundational philosophy articulated by Walz himself, suggesting that the act of consistently following through on and building ideas is critical for the continued generation of new ideas. He described it as a 'muscle you have to keep using,' implying that execution reinforces and fuels the entire creative cycle.
idea_generation_heuristics.example_projects: This is a meta-heuristic that applies to his entire body of work.

idea_backlog_and_management.description: The most direct public evidence of an idea backlog is a physical, visual system. A New York Times article from October 4, 2025, reported that Riley Walz's living room contains 'a wall of colorful Post-it notes with ideas he and his friends have brainstormed for an annual scavenger hunt they produce.' This indicates a process of externalizing ideas onto a physical board, which also facilitates collaborative brainstorming with friends. While the sheer volume of his projects suggests other informal lists may exist, the Post-it wall is the only explicitly documented backlog method found in public sources. The research found no public evidence of him using digital backlog tools like Trello or Notion.

project_selection_criteria.description: Walz's projects are frequently born from the discovery of a novel, accessible, or exploitable data source. He demonstrates a pattern of identifying and leveraging publicly available data streams, often through unconventional means like reverse-engineering, scraping, or exploiting system loopholes. The idea often seems to follow the data's availability.
project_selection_criteria.supporting_examples: Find My Parking Cops (reverse-engineered predictable ticket numbers from SFMTA's system), Looksmapping (scraped millions of Google Maps reviews and profile pictures), IMG_0001 (found and curated YouTube videos with default filenames), Library Spy (tracked public library checkout data), Amtrak Scraper (used a public API to show train occupancy).

project_selection_criteria.description: A strong preference is shown for projects that can be rapidly prototyped and launched, often within a weekend or a few weeks of part-time work. This 'ship fast' behavior suggests that the feasibility of quick execution is a major filter for which ideas are pursued. Projects are scoped minimally to demonstrate the core concept.
project_selection_criteria.supporting_examples: Find My Parking Cops (built in 'free time on the weekends the last few weeks'), Fake 2020 Candidate (created in under 30 minutes as a high-schooler), Jmail (initial prototype reportedly took about 5 hours), IMG_0001 (built over a couple of days, taking 'four or five hours total').

project_selection_criteria.description: Projects are often selected for their potential to generate social media buzz and press coverage. They typically possess a playful, provocative, absurd, or controversial quality that makes them inherently newsworthy. The framing often involves social commentary or a 'stunt' quality that captures public imagination.
project_selection_criteria.supporting_examples: Find My Parking Cops (went viral and received national press despite being live for only 4 hours), Mehran's Steak House (a fake restaurant that generated a 900-party waitlist and media coverage), Bop Spotter (quirky concept of a street-corner Shazam was covered by NPR), Looksmapping (provocative concept of rating attractiveness via AI was designed to spark discussion).

project_selection_criteria.description: A fundamental driver for Walz is personal curiosity and a sense of playfulness. Many projects are framed as experiments to 'see what would happen' or as a 'funny little joke.' This intrinsic motivation appears to be the foundational filter for his ideas, prioritizing concepts he personally finds cool or interesting.
project_selection_criteria.supporting_examples: Bop Spotter (Walz told KQED he 'just wanted to see what would happen' and 'thought it would be cool to hear what people are playing'), Postal Arbitrage (started as a fun way to interact with his family's group chat), General Statements (describes his work as being driven by wanting to 'see something cool' and sharing it).

project_selection_criteria.description: This criterion involves identifying and leveraging pricing discrepancies, logistical loopholes, or other non-obvious quirks in existing commercial or public systems. These projects turn systemic oddities into 'arbitrage' opportunities or social experiments.
project_selection_criteria.supporting_examples: Postal Arbitrage (identified that some Amazon Prime items with free shipping cost less than a postage stamp), Find My Parking Cops (exploited the predictable, sequential pattern of parking ticket citation numbers which was not an intended public API).

project_selection_criteria.description: A subset of projects involves deploying low-cost, custom-built physical sensors in the real world to capture continuous data. This transforms a physical space into a live data feed for observation and analysis.
project_selection_criteria.supporting_examples: Bop Spotter (a microphone, solar panel, and old phone pointed at a street to constantly Shazam music), Weather Watching (an AI model watching a public doorbell camera feed to count pedestrians and analyze their clothing).

hypothesized_project_choice_rubric.description: This is the foundational filter. The process begins with an idea that sparks personal curiosity, joy, or seems like a 'funny little joke.' If the concept isn't intrinsically interesting or cool to Walz, it is unlikely to be pursued. This is supported by his direct quotes emphasizing curiosity as a primary motivator for projects like Bop Spotter.

content.project_scoping_and_shipping_philosophy:
Riley Walz's project scoping and shipping philosophy is best described as the 'Minimum Viable Stunt.' This approach prioritizes speed, novelty, and public demonstration over polish, longevity, or feature completeness. His projects are scoped to be built quickly, often within a weekend or even a few hours, allowing him to maintain a high project velocity and consistently test new ideas.

**Key Principles of His Approach:**

*   **Rapid Prototyping and Shipping:** Walz is known for extremely short development cycles. He stated a project like IMG_0001 'took maybe four or five hours total,' and he has a habit of starting projects 'over the course of the weekend and just put them out there and see what happens.' This behavior indicates a strong preference for getting a functional concept into the public sphere as quickly as possible.

*   **Functionality Over Longevity (Embracing Fragility):** A core tenet of his philosophy is that a project is ready to ship when it demonstrates the core concept, even if it's fragile or its data source is ephemeral. The most prominent example is 'Find My Parking Cops,' which was launched despite its reliance on an unofficial data source. The project functioned for only four hours before the SFMTA cut off access. Rather than viewing this as a failure, the project's brief, viral life is presented as a successful 'stunt.' This demonstrates a willingness to trade robustness for speed and impact.

*   **Minimalist Scoping:** Projects are tightly scoped around a single, compelling function. For IMG_0001, he applied strict editorial filters (e.g., view counts, video length, upload date) to create a distinct experience without adding complex features. This minimalist approach ensures that the core idea is clear and the build time remains low.

*   **Shipping Triggers:** The decision to ship is triggered by several factors:
    1.  **Technical Milestone Achieved:** Once the core technical challenge is solved (e.g., reverse-engineering the parking ticket numbers, getting the Shazam sensor to log data), the project is considered ready for launch.
    2.  **Novelty and Curiosity:** Projects are often launched simply to satisfy a curiosity and 'see what would happen.' The launch itself is part of the experiment.
    3.  **Public Feedback:** Walz uses public launches to gauge audience reaction. He ships a minimal prototype and uses feedback from friends and the public to decide whether to iterate further or move on. The launch is not the end of the process but a critical data-gathering step.

*   **Iteration vs. Kill Criteria:** Post-launch, the decision to iterate, sustain, or kill a project appears to be based on a combination of public interest and external factors. 'Find My Parking Cops' was killed by an external dependency failure. 'Mehran’s Steak House' was iterated upon and scaled from a fake online listing to a real-world pop-up because social hype created a significant opportunity. Projects with commercial potential and team alignment, like Numerous.ai, are sustained and developed long-term, distinguishing them from his ephemeral 'stunts.'

data_sourcing_playbook.description: This tactic involves identifying and exploiting predictable patterns, vulnerabilities, or undocumented features in public-facing government or corporate systems to extract data in bulk. It often focuses on systems that lack formal APIs but expose data through sequential identifiers or predictable URL structures.
data_sourcing_playbook.example_projects: Find My Parking Cops: Walz discovered that San Francisco's parking ticket citation numbers were not random but followed a predictable pattern ('add 11, except add 4 if the last digit is 6'). He wrote an efficient scraper to query for sequential ticket numbers, allowing him to retrieve ticket data almost immediately after it was written.

data_sourcing_playbook.tactic: Large-Scale Scraping of Public Web Platforms
data_sourcing_playbook.description: This method involves systematically extracting large volumes of publicly available, user-generated content from major web platforms. The goal is to aggregate a massive dataset that can then be analyzed, often with AI, to derive novel or provocative insights.
data_sourcing_playbook.example_projects: Looksmapping: Walz scraped millions of restaurant reviews from Google Maps, specifically targeting the reviewers' profile pictures. This data was then fed into an AI model to rate attractiveness, creating a map of where 'hot' people dine. IMG_0001: He found and scraped millions of YouTube videos that had default camera filenames as titles (e.g., 'IMG_0276') to create a curated feed of unedited, 'accidental' content.

data_sourcing_playbook.description: This tactic involves placing inexpensive, custom-built sensor packages in the physical world to capture continuous, real-time data about the environment. These setups often use commodity hardware (like old phones), open-source software, and public Wi-Fi to stream data to a server for processing and publication.
data_sourcing_playbook.example_projects: Bop Spotter: Walz deployed a weatherproof box in San Francisco containing an old phone, a microphone, and a solar panel. The phone constantly runs the Shazam app to identify music playing on the street below, creating a real-time public log of the neighborhood's soundtrack. Weather Watching: This project used AI to watch a public Manhattan doorbell camera feed, analyzing the video to count pedestrians and identify what they were wearing (e.g., coats, umbrellas) to create an alternative, observational weather report.

data_sourcing_playbook.description: This involves identifying and leveraging logistical or pricing anomalies within large commercial systems. The goal is to create a novel service or social experiment based on these quirks.
data_sourcing_playbook.example_projects: Postal Arbitrage: Walz noticed that some items on Amazon Prime were sold for less than the cost of a U.S. postage stamp, with free shipping included. He created a site that aggregated these items, framing it as a form of 'postal arbitrage' for sending small, inexpensive physical gifts.

data_sourcing_playbook.description: This tactic focuses on accessing and making visible data that is technically public but often obscure or difficult to track. It involves tapping into the data streams of public institutions like libraries to reveal community patterns.
data_sourcing_playbook.example_projects: Library Spy: This project involved tracking and displaying a real-time log of books being checked out from a specific branch of the New York City library, making a typically non-public activity transparent.

data_sourcing_playbook.description: This method involves searching for and curating large sets of unintentionally public or overlooked digital content. By identifying content based on metadata patterns (like default filenames), he creates novel collections that offer unfiltered glimpses into digital life.
data_sourcing_playbook.example_projects: IMG_0001: This project is built entirely on this tactic, sourcing YouTube videos that were likely uploaded without a custom title. The result is a collection of 'unedited, pure moments from random lives.'

content.platform_responses_and_risk_mitigation:
Riley Walz's projects, which often operate in a gray area by leveraging unofficial data access, have elicited direct and swift responses from platforms and authorities. The most prominent example is his 'Find My Parking Cops' project. Within just four hours of its launch on September 23, 2025, the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (SFMTA) responded by altering its website and cutting off the data source Walz was using to track parking enforcement officers. Walz's reaction was not to escalate the situation but to document it, updating the project page to state: "This site only worked for 4 hours until the SFMTA cut the data source I was using."

In response to such risks, Walz has developed several mitigation strategies. A key tactic is the strategic withholding of operational details. Regarding his 'Bop Spotter' project, which uses a microphone to identify music on a San Francisco street, Walz explicitly told WIRED he conceals its location to prevent authorities from easily finding and removing it: "I haven't shared the location online or even a picture of what the box looks like, just because I don't want anyone to get too—for the city to have a very easy time finding it if they want to take it down." This demonstrates a deliberate choice to operate with a degree of obscurity to ensure project longevity.

Other risk mitigation techniques include:
- **Proactive Ethical Framing:** For projects with potential social or ethical backlash, like 'LooksMapping' (which used AI to rate the attractiveness of restaurant patrons), Walz includes a 'Methodology' section that directly addresses potential criticisms. He acknowledged the AI model was "certainly biased" and "flawed," framing the project as "a mirror held up to our collective vanity," thereby preempting some criticism by controlling the narrative.
- **Legal Disclaimers:** His project pages frequently feature clear disclaimers to manage legal exposure and user expectations. For example, 'Find My Parking Cops' stated it was "Not affiliated with the San Francisco government or Apple's Find My," and 'Postal Arbitrage' noted it was "not affiliated with or endorsed by Amazon, obviously!"

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: Project names are direct, simple, and immediately convey the core function. This ensures that potential users and media can grasp the concept instantly without needing a lengthy explanation. The name itself is the primary marketing tool.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: Projects like 'Find My Parking Cops,' 'LooksMapping,' and 'Postal Arbitrage' have names that are self-explanatory. 'Find My Parking Cops' clearly communicates that the tool helps locate parking enforcement officers.

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: Walz often uses puns or allusions to well-known technologies or cultural phenomena. This adds a layer of cleverness and memorability, and can quickly contextualize a project by relating it to something familiar.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: The name 'Bop Spotter' is a direct pun on 'ShotSpotter,' the acoustic gunshot detection system. This immediately frames the project as a form of surveillance, but for a playful purpose ('catching vibes' instead of criminals).

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: Nearly every project includes a prominent 'Methodology,' 'How it works,' or 'Read how I'm getting this data' link. This practice builds trust, satisfies technical curiosity, and allows him to control the narrative around his methods. It showcases his technical ingenuity and can preemptively answer questions.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: The 'LooksMapping' project page includes a 'Methodology' link. The 'Find My Parking Cops' page has a detailed 'How does this work?' section explaining the reverse-engineering of ticket numbers. 'Bop Spotter' details its hardware setup.

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: Projects are typically launched with a short, punchy, and often provocative announcement on social media, particularly X (formerly Twitter). The message is designed for high shareability and clearly states the project's utility or core hook.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: The launch tweet for 'Find My Parking Cops' was simply, 'I made a website. AVOID THE PARKING COPS,' followed by the link. This direct, benefit-oriented message is highly effective for generating immediate interest and virality.

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: For projects with potentially controversial or ethically ambiguous aspects, Walz proactively acknowledges and discusses these issues within his 'Methodology' sections. This demonstrates self-awareness and helps frame the project as a piece of social commentary rather than a naive or malicious tool.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: On the 'LooksMapping' page, he explicitly states, 'The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed... This website just puts reductive numbers on the superficial calculations we make every day. A mirror held up to our collective vanity.' This frames the project as a critique of societal biases.

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: Projects are framed to highlight a compelling, often mischievous or thought-provoking utility. The communication emphasizes the 'stunt' or 'hack' nature of the work, which makes it inherently more newsworthy.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: 'Bop Spotter' is framed not just as a music logger, but as 'culture surveillance.' This provocative framing generates intrigue and positions the project as a commentary on public privacy and data collection.

naming_and_communication_strategy.description: To manage legal exposure and user expectations, his project pages often include explicit disclaimers stating that they are not affiliated with the entities they interact with or reference.
naming_and_communication_strategy.example: The 'Find My Parking Cops' page included a disclaimer: 'Not affiliated with the San Francisco government or Apple's Find My.' The 'Postal Arbitrage' page notes, 'This site is not affiliated with or endorsed by Amazon, obviously!'

chronological_project_timeline.description: As a high-school student, Walz created a fake political candidate, 'Andrew Walz,' and successfully got the account verified by Twitter in under five minutes to demonstrate platform vulnerabilities ahead of the 2020 election.
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: The account was suspended by Twitter after media inquiries. The project successfully highlighted weaknesses in Twitter's verification process.

chronological_project_timeline.description: An elaborate prank that began as a fake high-end steakhouse listing on Google Maps, complete with fabricated reviews. The social media hype grew so large that it culminated in a real one-night-only pop-up restaurant in New York City.
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: The project successfully transitioned from an online joke to a real-world event, attracting over 100 diners and significant media coverage.

chronological_project_timeline.description: A website that curates and displays a feed of obscure YouTube videos that have default camera filenames (e.g., 'IMG_0001.MOV') as titles, offering 'unedited, pure moments from random lives.'
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: Ongoing. The project was exhibited at the Dordrechts Museum in 2025, gaining recognition as a piece of web art.

chronological_project_timeline.description: A physical installation in San Francisco's Mission District consisting of a solar-powered phone with a microphone that constantly uses Shazam to identify and log music being played on the street, described as 'culture surveillance' for 'catching vibes.'
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: Ongoing. The project's website showed active data logs from its launch through at least January 2026.

chronological_project_timeline.description: A commercial software-as-a-service product co-founded by Walz that integrates AI capabilities, like using ChatGPT, directly into spreadsheets such as Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel.
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: Ongoing. The service is live and represents a more sustained, product-focused venture compared to his other 'stunts'.

chronological_project_timeline.description: A real-time map that tracked the location of San Francisco parking enforcement officers by reverse-engineering the city's sequential parking ticket numbering system and scraping the data.
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: Worked for 4 hours. The live data feed was shut down by the SFMTA shortly after the project went viral.

chronological_project_timeline.description: A controversial project that cloned the Gmail interface and populated it with Jeffrey Epstein's publicly released emails, making them searchable and easily browsable. The initial prototype was built in about five hours.
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: The site received massive traffic (reportedly 18.4 million visits) and led to derivative projects ('J-suite'). It remained active for months.

chronological_project_timeline.description: A website that identifies items on Amazon Prime that, with free shipping, cost less than a U.S. postage stamp (78 cents in 2025), allowing users to send small physical items for less than the cost of a letter.
chronological_project_timeline.outcome_or_lifespan: Ongoing as a web page on his personal site.

content.analysis_of_project_velocity_and_scope_e:
Riley Walz's project history demonstrates a high velocity of rapid, small-scope experiments, with a clear evolution in technical complexity and ambition over time, culminating in a significant shift following his employment at OpenAI.

**Project Velocity and Time-to-Ship:**
Before joining OpenAI, Walz maintained a consistent cadence of public projects, with a conservative estimate of 1.5 to 2.0 major launches per year between 2020 and 2025. His development cycle is exceptionally fast, with a clear preference for projects that can be shipped quickly. This is evidenced by:
- **Rapid Prototypes (Hours):** The initial version of 'Jmail' was reportedly developed in just five hours.
- **Short Builds (Days to Weeks):** 'Find My Parking Cops' was built over a few weeks of weekend work. Other experiments like 'IMG_0001' were described as being completed over a couple of days. This 'ship fast' behavior, often materializing as 'weekend builds,' allows him to test ideas with minimal upfront investment.

**Evolution of Scope and Technical Complexity:**
Walz's projects show a distinct progression in scope and technical sophistication:
- **Early Phase (2020-2023):** His initial projects were primarily clever pranks and social experiments that exploited platform rules or social dynamics, such as the fake verified Twitter candidate (2020) and the fake Google Maps restaurant (2023). These required more social engineering than deep technical work.
- **Mid Phase (2024):** He began incorporating more complex technical elements, including physical hardware and data curation. 'Bop Spotter' involved creating a physical sensing device, while 'IMG_0001' required scraping and filtering large amounts of data from YouTube.
- **Late Phase (2025):** This period saw his most technically ambitious independent projects. 'Find My Parking Cops' involved reverse-engineering a government data system. 'Jmail' and its 'J-suite' expansions involved handling massive datasets, OCR, and AI-powered search, demonstrating a significant increase in scale and complexity compared to his earlier work.

**Shift After OpenAI Hiring:**
A major inflection point occurred in February 2026 when Walz was hired by OpenAI. In the period immediately following his hiring (February - May 2026), there was a notable decline in his public-facing project launches. While his pre-hire period was characterized by frequent, independent 'stunts,' the post-hire window shows no major new public projects. This strongly suggests a shift in focus from independent, public experimentation to internal, proprietary work at OpenAI, marking a significant change in his creative output and public persona.
content.role_of_external_validation_and_incentiv:
External validation—in the form of media coverage, social media virality, and career opportunities—appears to play a significant role as both an amplifier and a secondary incentive in Riley Walz's project choices and shipping decisions. While he often frames his work as being driven by pure curiosity, his methods and the outcomes of his projects show a strong correlation with public attention.

**Media Coverage and Virality as an Incentive:**
Walz's projects are frequently designed to be inherently newsworthy. Concepts like tracking parking police ('Find My Parking Cops'), rating diners' attractiveness ('LooksMapping'), or creating a 'culture surveillance' device ('Bop Spotter') are provocative and easily digestible for a wide audience. His launch strategy is highly effective at generating buzz: he typically announces a project with a single, concise, and often provocative post on X (formerly Twitter), which is optimized for shareability. 

This strategy has repeatedly proven successful. 'Find My Parking Cops' went viral within hours of his tweet, leading to immediate coverage from local outlets like *The San Francisco Standard* and, shortly after, a profile in *The New York Times* that dubbed him a 'Tech Jester.' This pattern suggests that the potential for press and social media engagement is a key consideration in his project selection. The names he chooses—short, descriptive, and often punny—are also perfect for headlines, further aligning his work with media appeal.

**Career Outcomes as Validation:**
The most significant external validation is the impact on his career. His public persona as a creative and technically adept 'prankster' directly contributed to his hiring by OpenAI in February 2026. Media outlets like *WIRED* explicitly drew a line between his reputation for building viral online stunts and his new role at the prestigious AI lab. Walz himself publicly announced the job on X, treating it as a shareable achievement, which garnered over 300,000 views. This demonstrates that the visibility gained from his public projects translated into a tangible and highly desirable career outcome, reinforcing the value of creating high-visibility work.

While Walz's intrinsic motivation and a maker's curiosity are evident, the consistent alignment of his projects with what generates press, the optimization of his launch strategy for virality, and the clear, positive impact on his career strongly suggest that external validation is a powerful force shaping his project choices and timing.
content.evidence_vs_inference_summary:
Riley Walz's project model can be understood through a combination of directly evidenced practices and strongly inferred heuristics based on observable patterns.

**Heuristics Supported by Direct Evidence (High Confidence):**
These are practices confirmed by Walz's own words, project pages, or direct reporting.
*   **Maintaining a Physical Idea Backlog:** Evidence comes from a *New York Times* article (Oct 4, 2025) that reported on and photographed a wall of Post-it notes in his living room used for brainstorming ideas.
*   **Curiosity as a Primary Motivator:** Walz explicitly stated this in interviews. For 'Bop Spotter,' he told KQED, "I had an idea for Bop Spotter and just wanted to see what would happen." He described his projects to eWeek as a way to "exercise his curiosity."
*   **Rapid Prototyping and Weekend Builds:** He told *WIRED* that 'Find My Parking Cops' was built in his "free time on the weekends the last few weeks." His creation of a fake verified Twitter candidate in under 30 minutes as a high-schooler also demonstrates this habit.
*   **Reverse-Engineering and Scraping:** His methodology page for 'Find My Parking Cops' explicitly details how he reverse-engineered the ticket numbering system. The 'Looksmapping' page states, "I scraped millions of Google Maps restaurant reviews."
*   **'Show Your Work' Transparency:** His project websites consistently feature 'Methodology' or 'How it works' sections, providing direct evidence of his commitment to explaining his process.
*   **Belief in Consistent Execution:** He was quoted by eWeek saying, "You have to follow through on your ideas because if you don't, you might stop having them. It's like a muscle you have to keep using."

**Heuristics Inferred from Observed Patterns (Medium to High Confidence):**
These are conclusions drawn from the consistent outcomes and characteristics of his projects.
*   **A Comprehensive Decision Rubric (Medium-High Confidence):** Researchers inferred a multi-step decision rubric based on his project choices: 1) Is it interesting? 2) Is data available? 3) Can it be built fast? 4) Does it have viral potential? 5) Is the risk manageable? This rubric is not explicitly stated by Walz but is a highly consistent pattern across his work.
*   **Optimizing for Novelty and Press (Medium-High Confidence):** The consistent pattern of projects with provocative angles, launched with media-friendly social media posts, and resulting in significant press coverage strongly suggests an optimization for public attention. While he doesn't state this as a goal, the correlation is strong and noted by multiple outlets. The fact that his viral stunts were linked to his hiring at OpenAI provides further support for this inferred incentive.
*   **The 'Minimum Viable Stunt' Philosophy (High Confidence):** The concept of shipping a project when it's just functional enough to demonstrate the core idea is an inference, but a very strong one. The 4-hour lifespan of 'Find My Parking Cops' and the rapid launch of other weekend projects are powerful examples of prioritizing the 'stunt' or demonstration over longevity and polish.

key_projects_overview.description: A short-lived website that tracked the real-time location of San Francisco parking enforcement officers by scraping the city's ticketing system.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: Reverse-Engineering Public Systems: Exploiting predictable patterns in a public government data system to create a novel information service.

key_projects_overview.description: A physical installation of a microphone and a phone on a street corner that constantly uses Shazam to identify and log music being played by passing cars and pedestrians.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: Playful Physical-World Sensing: Using low-cost consumer technology to capture and publish real-time cultural data from the environment out of curiosity.

key_projects_overview.description: A map that rates the attractiveness of a restaurant's clientele by scraping millions of Google Maps reviews and using an AI to rate the reviewers' profile pictures.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: AI-Enhanced Public Data Analysis: Applying AI to a large, publicly scraped dataset to generate novel and provocative social insights.

key_projects_overview.description: A tool that finds items on Amazon Prime that cost less to ship for free than the price of a postage stamp, enabling a form of gift-giving arbitrage.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: Exploiting Systemic Inefficiencies: Identifying and leveraging a pricing and logistics anomaly in a major commercial service for a creative purpose.

key_projects_overview.description: A website that curates and displays a random feed of millions of YouTube videos that have default camera filenames as titles, offering unedited glimpses of everyday life.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: Curating Accidental Digital Artifacts: Finding and presenting overlooked or unintentionally public content to create a unique, raw viewing experience.

key_projects_overview.description: An elaborate prank that began as a fake high-end restaurant on Google Maps, which generated so much hype that it culminated in a real one-night pop-up event.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: Social Engineering and Platform Exploitation: Leveraging the mechanics of a public platform (Google Maps reviews) and social hype to blur the line between digital fiction and reality.

key_projects_overview.description: A project that tracks and publicly displays the books being checked out in real-time from a specific branch of the New York Public Library.
key_projects_overview.primary_heuristic_demonstrated: Transparency through Public Record Monitoring: Making technically public but obscure institutional data easily accessible to reveal community patterns.

key_heuristics_summary.description: A bulleted list of the core, repeatable heuristics that define Riley Walz's entire project lifecycle, from ideation to launch.
